## Mesora & Sefer-Torah Fixing

### 121. What parts of the Aleppo Codex survive, and what is the Leningrad Codex?

The Aleppo Codex (כֶּתֶר אֲרָם צוֹבָא) was the oldest and most authoritative Masoretic manuscript of the Tanach. A riot in 1947 destroyed or lost approximately one-third of it, including nearly all of the Torah (only the last few chapters survive), most of Writings, and parts of the Prophets. What remains is still invaluable, especially for Nevi'im. The Leningrad Codex (dated 1008 CE) is the oldest complete manuscript of the entire Tanach, and serves as the base text for the Biblia Hebraica Stuttgartensia and most modern printed editions. For books where the Aleppo Codex survives, it is considered superior; where it does not, the Leningrad is the best available.

**Sources:**

- ~ MJ #msg2552
- ~ MG #msg1346

**See also:**

- [Who are the ba'alei mesora (Masoretes), and what exactly did they do?](#baalei-mesorah-who)
- [Which seforim are used to look up Masorah lists (e.g. missing mappikim, exceptional readings)?](#mesora-reference-works)

### 122. Who are the ba'alei mesora (Masoretes), and what exactly did they do?

The term בַּעֲלֵי הַמָּסוֹרָה covers a range of scholars over approximately 300 years (c. 6th–9th centuries CE), most of whose identities are no longer known. They collectively developed the written notation system for vowels (nikud) and cantillation marks (trop), created lists and notes of how many times certain words or patterns appear in Tanach, and standardized the text. The two most prominent families are Ben Asher (BA) and Ben Naftali (BN), whose readings sometimes diverge. The Aleppo Codex (Keter Aram Tzova) is the most important surviving manuscript of the Ben Asher tradition; Yeivin's work documents the differences between BA and BN. The Leningrad Codex (the base text for most modern editions) is also Ben Asher. Minchat Shai consulted multiple codices in reaching his conclusions, and Rav Breuer's Chorev Tanach is a synthetic edition attempting to reconstruct the BA text, with his own methodological choices (e.g. removing all chataf patach under non-gutturals and replacing with shva).

**Examples:**

- Ben Asher vs Ben Naftali divergences (e.g. כִּי־מִי נַח זֹאת לִי in the haftara of כִּי תָצֵא)

**Sources:**

- ~ MJ #msg2585
- ~ Dror Maor #msg2545
- ~ MG #msg2550
- ~ MJ #msg2552
- ~ MG #msg2554
- ~ Moish #msg4059
- ~ MJ #msg4060

**See also:**

- [Which seforim are used to look up Masorah lists (e.g. missing mappikim, exceptional readings)?](#mesora-reference-works)
- [When different chumashim/tikkunim show different nikkud or trop, is that a 'machloket' (dispute)?](#girsa-vs-machloket)

### 123. If a rav or rosh yeshiva makes a mistake in leining (or haftarah), should you correct them?

A real dilemma discussed in the group. Logically one should correct, but doing so publicly can lessen people's respect for the Rav. The prevailing sentiment leans toward שֵׁב וְאַל תַּעֲשֶׂה (sit and do not act), i.e. not correcting, because embarrassing a Rav publicly may be worse than the mistake itself. There is a general observation that not every rosh yeshiva knows all the fine details of dikduk and te'amim, and one also cannot always distinguish what is correct לִכְתַּחִלָּה from what someone may have considered not worth protesting בִּדְעָבַד.

**Sources:**

- ~ Moshe Klausner #msg1860
- ~ Simcha Simon #msg1861
- ~ Moshe Klausner #msg1862
- ~ MJ #msg1854

**See also:**

- [When different chumashim/tikkunim show different nikkud or trop, is that a 'machloket' (dispute)?](#girsa-vs-machloket)

### 124. Why does Eicha have a unique layout with each pasuk on its own line (the 'windows' format)?

The Sofrim call this minhag of writing the first four chapters of Eicha with a פַּרְשָׁה סְתוּמָה (closed paragraph break) between each pasuk 'חֲלוֹנוֹת חֲלוֹנוֹת' (windows). This is how the manuscripts have it as well. We do not have the Aleppo Codex for Eicha, but the Leningrad Codex does have it — with Chapter 3 written with a stuma between each triple (not each individual pasuk), matching the triple-acrostic structure. Chapter 5 is not written this way. The Tikkun Simanim reproduces this layout, which can surprise people who have only seen Eicha written like a regular megillah. Sofrim can produce a klaf in this format on request, though it is more expensive.

**Sources:**

- Yona Kesler #msg1345
- ~ MG #msg1346
- ~ Dovy Pollack #msg1347
- ~ MG #msg1348

**See also:**

- [When leining Eicha (or the Eicha tune), should the etnachta and sof pasuk be sung differently, and does anyone differentiate the tipcha before etnachta from the tipcha before sof pasuk?](#eicha-tune-etnachta-vs-sof-pasuk)

### 125. When different chumashim/tikkunim show different nikkud or trop, is that a 'machloket' (dispute)?

Usually it is better described as different GIRSA'OT (textual versions) rather than a 'machloket.' Better manuscripts and editions tend to converge on a reading (e.g. a revia on וְאַנִּי in Vayeitzei appears in the superior mss/editions, where a weaker tikkun might show gershayim). The Masorah sometimes explicitly gives the sofer/editor OPTIONS (e.g. לֻקְחָה may be written with a shva or as chataf kamatz — there are manuscripts with just a shva that Minchat Shai did not have). Crucially, such variations often do NOT change pronunciation (e.g. shva vs chataf-kamatz on לֻקְחָה sounds the same); they reflect scribal/masoretic notation choices. So 'why does my tikkun differ?' is usually answered by 'different girsa/edition,' and the question becomes which has the stronger manuscript basis.

**Examples:**

- לֻקְחָה — shva or chataf-kamatz (sounds identical; a masoretic-notation option)
- revia vs gershayim on וְאַנִּי — better mss have revia

**Sources:**

- ~ Ezra Arovas #msg927
- ~ MG #msg928
- ~ Dror Maor #msg775
- ~ MG #msg779
- ~ Dror Maor #msg781

**See also:**

- [Should it be לִסְבֹב or לִסְבֹּב (lisvov / lisbov)? Why is there a rafeh (no dagesh)?](#kamatz-katan-lisvov)
- [Which seforim are used to look up Masorah lists (e.g. missing mappikim, exceptional readings)?](#mesora-reference-works)

### 126. Why is 'hee' (she) almost always spelled הוא (masculine 'he') in Tanach?

The feminine pronoun 'הִיא' (she) is surprisingly almost always written as 'הוּא' (he) in the consonantal text of Tanach — it is one of the most common kri/ketiv patterns. The ketiv (written form) uses the masculine vav, but the kri (reading tradition) pronounces it with a chirik as 'hee'.

There are only a handful of exceptions where it is written fully as הִיא (with a yud), and these are noteworthy precisely because they deviate from the norm.

The explanation relates to the Masoretic tradition: the spelling with vav may reflect an archaic or dialectal pronunciation, or it may be a deliberate Masoretic device to indicate something about the word's grammatical function. Like all kri/ketiv, this is a matter of mesorah — the tradition is received and must be followed even if the written form seems inconsistent.

Other well-known kri/ketiv examples follow the same pattern: the written text may differ from the reading tradition in ways that seem inconsistent, but the ba'al k'riah always follows the kri (reading).

**Examples:**

- הוּא (ketiv) → הִיא (kri) — 'she', most occurrences in Tanach
- The few instances spelled הִיא (with yud) are the exceptions that prove the rule

**Sources:**

- ~ ~ #msg612
- ~ Moish #msg613

**See also:**

- [Why do aleph and resh sometimes have a dagesh when they normally cannot take one, as in תביאו and ערומים?](#dagesh-anomalous)

### 127. Which seforim are used to look up Masorah lists (e.g. missing mappikim, exceptional readings)?

Several layers: (1) אָכְלָה וְאָכְלָה — which Rashi calls the מְסֹרֶת הַגְּדוֹלָה (note: a different work from what we call the מָסוֹרָה גְּדוֹלָה). The common accessible edition is Frensdorff's. (2) C.D. Ginsburg's המסורה — massive and comprehensive, but not always easy to find the specific item. (3) The Aleppo Codex's own Masorah Gedolah (parsha-specific marginal notes, e.g. the conditions for shva na under וְשָׂבְתָה in Shoftim). Note that אָכְלָה וְאָכְלָה lists only UNIQUE words missing a feature (so a common word like לָהּ would not appear in its mappik-less list).

**Sources:**

- ~ MG #msg1893
- ~ MG #msg1894
- ~ MG #msg1895
- ~ MG #msg779
- ~ MG #msg674

**See also:**

- [Are there places where a mappik heh is expected but missing, and where can one find the lists?](#dagesh-mapik-omitted-list)

### 128. Does the 'mesora' include pronunciation (e.g. Ashkenazi vs Israeli vs Sephardi), or is that just dialect and accent?

A recurring philosophical debate. One position: every community historically spoke Hebrew with the accent of its host country (German Jews with a German accent, Spanish with Spanish, Arabic with Arabic, etc.) — that is simply the way of language, and one community's accent is not more 'mesora' than another's. The counter-position: there ARE authentic traditions of pronunciation (e.g. distinguishing ח/כ, ע/ג, ת/ס, or the Ashkenazi 'oy' cholam) that are part of the mesora and should be preserved. The group generally acknowledges that modern Israeli Hebrew was deliberately constructed by non-religious people who tried to uproot traditional pronunciation, which complicates its claim to 'authenticity.' That said, Israeli pronunciation is the dominant living tradition and must be respected in practice. The practical conclusion for most: be consistent in whatever tradition you follow, and do not try to 'correct' someone else's mesora.

**Sources:**

- ~ Dror Maor #msg479
- Binyomin (Ben) Adler #msg481
- ~ R Abraham #msg509
- ~ R Abraham #msg510
- ~ MJ #msg463
- ~ ~ #msg466

**See also:**

- [What is the history of the 'oy' vs 'oh' pronunciation of the cholam (e.g. ha-OY-lam vs ha-OH-lam)?](#cholam-oy-vs-oh)
- [Why do Teimanim pronounce ג 'in reverse' (soft/dageshed gimel), and is it only gimel?](#teimani-gimel)

### 129. Which tikkun/chumash edition is considered most accurate, and how do they differ?

Several editions are commonly discussed: (1) תִּקּוּן סִימָנִים (Simanim) — the most widely used tikkun among serious ba'alei koreh; newer versions sometimes have minor updates. (2) תּוֹרַת חַיִּים / חוֹרֶב (Rav Breuer) — a synthetic reconstruction of the Ben Asher text. Notable methodological choices: all chataf patach under non-gutturals replaced with shva; changes to some גַּעְיוֹת הָרְשׁוּת; overall considered very accurate but represents Breuer's editorial judgment in some places. (3) אִישׁ מַצְלִיחַ — generally reliable but differs from Simanim in some readings (e.g. sometimes has a pesik where Simanim has meteg). (4) Artscroll / Koren — widely used but considered less precise than Simanim or Chorev in nikud and trop. Koren is generally considered slightly better than Artscroll. For pure manuscript fidelity, Breuer's Chorev and the original Aleppo Codex photos (what survives) are the gold standard. The Berditchev Tikkun was endorsed by the GRA but does not conform in many places to the Aleppo Codex.

### 130. What is the תּוֹרַת הַקּוֹרֵא (Torat HaKore) approach to pronunciation, and how does it differ from traditional mesora?

The תּוֹרַת הַקּוֹרֵא organization advocates for pronunciation changes where the meaning would change if you keep to the 'incorrect' traditional version — e.g. differentiating ח and כ when the meaning of a word depends on it. Its approach is based on the idea that correct meaning is paramount. However, the group generally reacted skeptically: 'חֲדָשִׁים מִקָּרוֹב בָּאוּ' (new things are close to coming) — creating a new mesora based on what 'we think is right' is seen as problematic. Traditionalists would say that the living mesora received from previous generations, even if it has evolved, carries weight that a newly constructed pronunciation cannot match.

**Sources:**

- ~ Moish #msg5440
- ~ Dror Maor #msg5441
- ~ Dror Maor #msg5443
- ~ Dror Maor #msg5444

## Names & Lishonot

### 131. Why is the word אדני (Ad-nai) in Tanach sometimes vocalized with a kamatz under the nun (אֲדֹנָי) and sometimes with a patach (אֲדֹנַי)? Does it change the meaning?

Yes — the difference in vocalization reflects a difference in meaning:

**Kamatz under the nun (אֲדֹנָי) = Shem Hashem (Divine name)**
- When the word refers to Hashem, it is vocalized with a KAMATZ under the nun
- This is the standard vocalization for אדני as one of the names of Hashem
- Example: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲדֹנָי אִם־נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ (Bereishis 18:3)

**Patach under the nun (אֲדֹנַי) = chol / 'my masters'**
- When the word means 'my masters' (chol, l'chol), it is vocalized with a PATACH under the nun
- Example: אֲדֹנַי שְׁמָעוּנִי (Bereishis 23:11) — 'my masters, listen to me' (Avraham speaking to the Bnei Ches)

**Confusion cases:**
- Rashi sometimes comments explicitly on which meaning applies
- Some cases are a machlokes between Rashi and Onkelos — the trop and context guide the interpretation
- Ezra Arovas: 'Rashi says that it depends on the meaning. Sometimes it means Hashem, and sometimes it's chol ("my masters")'
- Dror Maor: 'Meaning, with a kamatz can still be chol and it just depends on context?' — the answer is yes, the kamatz/patach is a strong indicator but context is also important

**Exception — pausal forms:**
- In some cases, a pausal form can affect the vowel, but the kamatz/patach distinction is primarily a meaning marker, not just a pausal phenomenon
- When both have a revii (as Ezra noted: Bereishis 18:3 with kamatz vs 19:2 with patach), the difference is clearly semantic, not pausal

**For ba'alei k'riah:**
- Pay attention to the nikkud — it tells you whether the word means 'Hashem' or 'my masters'
- If you're unsure, the context usually clarifies the meaning

**Examples:**

- אֲדֹנָי (kamatz) = Shem Hashem — e.g., וַיֹּאמֶר אֲדֹנָי (Bereishis 18:3)
- אֲדֹנַי (patach) = 'my masters' (chol) — e.g., אֲדֹנַי שְׁמָעוּנִי (Bereishis 23:11)
- Confusion cases exist — Rashi and Onkelos sometimes disagree on which meaning applies

**Sources:**

- ~ Ezra Arovas #msg5407
- ~ Dror Maor #msg5408
- ~ Dovid H #msg5412
- ~ Moish #msg5413
- ~ MJ #msg5415

**See also:**

- [Is a chataf kamatz always a kamatz katan (KK)? And how should a chataf that indicates neither shva-na nor KK be pronounced?](#kamatz-katan-vs-chataf)
- [What are pausal forms and why do they sometimes seem to 'override' the plain meaning?](#pausal-forms)

### 132. Is it proper to say 'Adoshem' instead of 'Adonai' when not davening? What do the Kitzur Shulchan Aruch and Mishna Berura say?

There is a discussion about whether saying 'Adoshem' (a portmanteau of Ad-nai + Hashem) is an appropriate way to refer to Hashem's name when not in a formal davening context.

**The Kitzur Shulchan Aruch's view:**
- Kitzur SA Siman 6, end of se'if 3: 'When one wants to mention the Name, he should say the word "Hashem" — and not like the common people who err and say "Adoshem", because this is not a way of respect for Above'
- The Kitzur explicitly discourages saying 'Adoshem' — he considers it disrespectful

**The Mishna Berura's silence:**
- Reuven Levitin noted: 'The Kitzur says it's not appropriate, but the MB makes no mention of this inyan'
- This raises the question: when the Kitzur says something and the Mishna Berura is silent, do we assume the MB agrees (שתיקה כהודאה) or not?
- Some argue that since the MB doesn't mention it, it's not a significant concern

**The practical common practice:**
- 'Adoshem' is very commonly used in Orthodox communities as a way to avoid saying 'Adonai' outside of davening
- Many consider it a respectful euphemism
- The Kitzur's objection seems to be that it's a 'made-up' word that combines the two names improperly
- The alternative that the Kitzur prefers is simply saying 'Hashem' (or 'Elokim' for Elokim)

**For ba'alei k'riah:**
- When referring to Hashem's name outside of davening, saying 'Hashem' is the most universally accepted form
- 'Adoshem' is very common but has some halachic opposition
- When learning or quoting pesukim outside of davening, say 'Hashem' (or 'Adonai' if you hold it's fine) — but be consistent with your practice
- The safest approach: use 'Hashem' alone, avoiding the compound form entirely

**Examples:**

- Kitzur SA: say 'Hashem', not 'Adoshem' — not a derech kavod
- Mishna Berura: no mention of this issue (silence = agreement?)
- Common practice: 'Adoshem' widely used despite Kitzur's objection
- MJ: 'When you quote a pasuk, please cite the site where it's cited'

### 133. Why is בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע sometimes written as שָׁבַע (shava) with a kamatz and sometimes as שֶׁבַע (sheva) with a segol? What does Seforno say about it?

The name בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע (Be'er Sheva) appears in Tanach with two different vocalizations of the second word — שֶׁבַע (sheva, with segol) and שָׁבַע (shava, with kamatz). The difference is debated.

**The simple grammatical explanation (Avi Herzog):**
- 'Shava with a kamatz is simply the pausal form of sheva with a segol'
- Every time the word appears in a pausal position (at etnachta, sof passuk, etc.), the segol naturally lengthens to a kamatz
- This is the same phenomenon as other pausal shifts (e.g., וַיֹּאמֶר → וַיֹּאמַר)
- The name itself is always 'Be'er Sheva' — the vocalization varies based on its position in the pasuk

**Seforno's explanation:**
- Seforno distinguishes between the two forms on semantic grounds
- שֶׁבַע with a segol means 'seven' and also 'oath' (shevu'ah)
- שָׁבַע with a kamatz is simply the pausal form (or could be understood differently)
- Moish elaborated: 'Seforno is coming to answer why we needed to name a place that already had that name. He's answering that שָׁבַע is NOT just the pausal form of שֶׁבַע — rather, the name was actually changed'
- According to Seforno, until Yitzchak renamed it, the name was בְּאֵר שָׁבַע (with kamatz even in non-pausal positions). Yitzchak changed it to בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע (with segol) to connote both the oath (shevu'ah) and the number seven

**Avi Herzog's critique of Seforno:**
- 'I find Seforno's explanation problematic — and unnecessary, because shava with a kamatz is simply the pausal form of sheva with a segol'
- 'Shava with a kamatz doesn't have a closer relationship to shevu'ah than Sheva with a segol'
- Moish countered: 'Who says one has to be closer to shevu'ah? Maybe they're equally close, just that sheva with segol has an added meaning of seven'

**For ba'alei k'riah:**
- Read the nikkud as written — the kamatz vs segol is determined by the ta'am (pause) or by the manuscript tradition
- The meaning of the name (the well of the oath/seven) is consistent regardless of vocalization
- Be aware of the machlokes between the simple pausal explanation and Seforno's interpretation

**Examples:**

- בְּאֵר שָׁבַע (kamatz) — pausal form OR deliberate name per Seforno
- בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע (segol) — non-pausal form, also connotes 'seven' per Seforno
- Avi Herzog: 'shava with kamatz is simply the pausal form'

**Sources:**

- ~ Dino #msg5718
- ~ Avi Herzog #msg5719
- ~ Moish #msg5722

**See also:**

- [What are pausal forms and why do they sometimes seem to 'override' the plain meaning?](#pausal-forms)
- [There are words where different editions show a segol vs a tzeire (e.g., בנזק, מלמטה). Which is correct and how do you determine?](#segol-vs-tzeire)

### 134. Is the name Rachel (רָחֵל) pronounced mil'eil or milra, and does the Tanach form differ from common usage?

Most people name their daughter רָחֵל mil'eil, but the name as it appears in the Torah is milra (רָחֵל with final stress). This is one of several cases where the common/colloquial pronunciation of a name differs from its Tanach form. Note this is about PERSON names; the same spelling can carry different stress. (Related recurring group discussions: לֵב/לֲיְבּ — Yiddish names follow Yiddish, not loshon haKodesh, dikduk; מִיכַל the boy's name has a shva under the chaf while מִיכָל the girl's name does not, and they are genuinely two different names.)

**Examples:**

- רָחֵל — Torah form is milra; common usage is mil'eil

**Sources:**

- Binyomin Abramson 89 #msg92
- ~ MJ #msg50

**See also:**

- [What is nasog achor (נסוג אחור), and when a word 'retreats' its accent, why does it sometimes happen even when the next word looks milra?](#nasog-achor)

### 135. Why is the name Mordechai (מרדכי) sometimes pronounced with a chirik under the כ (Mordechi) and sometimes with a kamatz (Mordechai)? Does the nikkud change depending on the context?

The name מרדכי (Mordechai) appears in Tanach with a chataf kamatz under the dalet and a chirik under the chaf: מָרְדֳּכַי (Mor-de-chai). However, in other contexts (like the sefer 'Mordechai' or the hagahos Mordechai), it sometimes appears with a plain shva under the dalet and a chirik under the chaf: מָרְדְּכִי (Mor-de-chi).

**The Tanach pronunciation:**
- In Megillas Esther, the name is always מָרְדֳּכַי with a chataf kamatz under the dalet — 'Mordechai'
- This is the standard Masoretic vocalization

**In sefer names and other contexts:**
- The sefer 'Mordechai' (by the R' Mordechai bar Hillel) is sometimes referred to with a chirik under the chaf — 'Mordechi'
- Similarly, the הגהות מרדכי (Hagahos Mordechai) is sometimes pronounced 'Hagahos Mordechi'
- Dovid H noted: 'Actually yes. As you would pronounce אשרי, for the sefer written by the רא"ש'
- The comparison is to 'Ashrei' (אשרי) — the sefer of the Rosh

**Why the difference?**
- It's similar to the distinction between 'Peretz' (פֶּרֶץ) and 'Partzi' (פַּרְצִי) — a name can shift vowels depending on grammatical context
- Unlike the Peretz/Partzi case, the Mordechai/Mordechi difference is NOT a pausal form phenomenon
- Rather, it reflects different naming traditions — the sefer name may have developed its own pronunciation over time

**Is there a rule?**
- In Tanach, the name is Mordechai (chataf kamatz under dalet, chirik under chaf) — follow the nikkud in Esther
- In rabbinic literature (Mordechai the sefer), the chirik pronunciation is also accepted
- The chirik pronunciation may reflect Ashkenazi influence or a separate diminutive form
- The two pronunciations don't reflect different meanings — they're the same name in different contexts

**For ba'alei k'riah:**
- When reading Megillas Esther, always say Mordechai (chataf kamatz under dalet, chirik under chaf) — that's what's written in the klaf
- When referring to the sefer 'Mordechai', both pronunciations are heard, with 'Mordechi' being common among yeshivish speakers
- The chataf kamatz form is the original Masoretic vocalization

**Examples:**

- מָרְדֳּכַי (kamatz) — in Megillas Esther, Masoretic tradition
- מָרְדְּכִי (chirik) — in the sefer name (Hagahos Mordechi), common in Yeshivish
- Compare: אשרי (Ashrei, sefer of Rosh) — similar chirik ending

**Sources:**

- Josh #msg5390
- ~ MG #msg5392
- ~ MG #msg5397

**See also:**

- [What are pausal forms and why do they sometimes seem to 'override' the plain meaning?](#pausal-forms)
- [In names like ישמעאל and דניאל, why is the tzeire under the ayin/yud and not under the aleph?](#tzeire-placement-names)

### 136. What is nasog achor (נסוג אחור), and when a word 'retreats' its accent, why does it sometimes happen even when the next word looks milra?

Nasog achor is the rule that when a mil'eil word is followed by a milra (or one-syllable) word, the accent of the first word 'retreats' to an earlier syllable so the two stresses don't collide. A subtlety: a word can BE milra for nasog-achor purposes even if it begins with a shva na (i.e. it is a single syllable word, or effectively one). So אִם לִמְקָרָא וְלִמְסֹרֶת — 'how can it be nasog achor if the next word is not mil'eil?' — it IS mil'eil (really a single-syllable word) even though it starts with a shva na; there are many nasog achor cases of this sort. Nasog achor rarely occurs with קַדְמָא וְאַזְלָא — something about how the tunes of those ta'amim make the words bind together. If we knew the exact original tunes we would likely understand why perfectly.

**Examples:**

- נסוג אחור rarely occurs with קַדְמָא וְאַזְלָא

**Sources:**

- ~ Σliezer Bulka #msg403
- ~ MJ #msg404
- ~ Ezra Arovas #msg905
- ~ Ezra Arovas #msg906
- ~ MJ #msg908

**See also:**

- [Is the name Rachel (רָחֵל) pronounced mil'eil or milra, and does the Tanach form differ from common usage?](#mil'eil-vs-milra)

### 137. In names like ישמעאל and דניאל, why is the tzeire under the ayin/yud and not under the aleph?

In compound names ending in אל (meaning 'God'), the tzeirei is under the letter before the lamed, not under the aleph of אל. For example:
- ישמעאל (Yishmael): the tzeirei is under the ayin, not under the aleph
- דניאל (Daniel): the tzeirei is under the yud, not under the aleph
- גבריאל, מיכאל, רפאל: all have the tzeirei under the letter preceding the lamed

This is because the name אל (El) is already a complete word with a segol under the aleph. When it serves as a suffix in compound names, the tzeirei is part of the preceding syllable, not part of the אל suffix. The first 'el' in Yishmael comes from 'shama' (heard) + 'El' — the ayin of 'shama' takes the tzeirei because of the pausal/lengthened form before the divine name component.

If the tzeirei were under the aleph, the ayin would need a vowel — either a shva na (which gutturals cannot take) or a chataf (which would be awkward before a tzeirei).

**Examples:**

- ישמעאל — tzeirei under ayin, segol under aleph (Yishma-el, not Yishm-ael)
- דניאל — tzeirei under yud, segol under aleph
- גבריאל, מיכאל, רפאל — same pattern: tzeirei before the lamed, segol under the aleph of אל

**Sources:**

- ~ Sholom Heidingsfeld CMLA #msg12567
- ~ Avi Herzog #msg12568
- ~ Elliot Pisem #msg12579

### 138. Is there a minhag to read יִשָּׂשכָר (Yissachar) the first time with two sins, or should the second sin be silent? What does the Masoretic tradition say?

The name Yissachar (יִשָּׂשכָר) has a complex masoretic history with a three-way dispute among the Ba'alei HaMasorah.

**The three masoretic opinions:**
1. **Ben Asher** (our tradition): יִשָּׂשכָר with a kamatz and dagesh in the first sin, with the second sin SILENT — effectively pronounced 'Yissachar' (one sin sound)
2. **Ben Naftali**: יִשְׁשָׂכָר with a SHIN (sh) with sheva nach followed by SIN (s) with kamatz — pronounced 'Yish-sachar'
3. **R' Moshe Mucha** (earlier Masorete): יִשְׂשָׂכָר with SIN with sheva nach followed by SIN with kamatz — sounds very close to Ben Asher's reading

**The modern minhag:**
- The Chasam Sofer reports a later minhag to read some instances of Yissachar with two sins (יִשָׂשְׂכָר, kamatz first then shva)
- This reading is NOT mentioned in the Sefer HaChilufim (which documents the three masoretic opinions)
- MJ: 'The reading יִשָׂשְׂכָר with kamatz first and then sheva is not mentioned at all, and the minhag to read some instances this way is of much more recent origin'
- The Chasam Sofer himself says this is a 'late minhag and not a valid Masoretic tradition'

**The practical tradition:**
- In all instances of the name, Ben Asher's tradition is the same — one sin sound with dagesh
- The Sefer HaChilufim mentions specifically that the three Ba'alei HaMasorah each held to their respective opinions in ALL instances of the name
- There is NO distinction between 'first time' and 'subsequent times' in any masoretic source
- The distinction between first and subsequent occurrences is a folk minhag, not a masoretic one

**For ba'alei k'riah:**
- The standard Masoretic reading is Yissachar (one sin sound) in all instances
- The minhag to read the first occurrence with two sin sounds is a later innovation, not required
- If you follow this minhag, be aware it's not based on any masoretic source
- The Ben Asher tradition (which we follow) reads it the same way every time: יִשָּׂשכָר with dagesh in the first sin and the second silent

**Examples:**

- Ben Asher: יִשָּׂשכָר — one sin with dagesh, second silent (our tradition)
- Ben Naftali: יִשְׁשָׂכָר — shin+sheva + sin+kamatz
- R' Moshe Mucha: יִשְׂשָׂכָר — sin+sheva + sin+kamatz
- Later minhag: יִשָׂשְׂכָר — kamatz + shva (not masoretic)

**Sources:**

- ~ Hillel Shapiro #msg6066
- ~ MG #msg6068
- ~ MG #msg6072

**See also:**

- [Why is 'hee' (she) almost always spelled הוא (masculine 'he') in Tanach?](#kri-ketiv-hou)
- [Which seforim are used to look up Masorah lists (e.g. missing mappikim, exceptional readings)?](#mesora-reference-works)

## Tefilla (Nusach & Teamim in Davening)

### 139. In the bracha אָב הָרַחֲמָן / אַב הָרַחֲמִים, should אָב have a kamatz or a patach?

Both occur in different siddurim because the phrases mean different things: (1) אָב הָרַחֲמָן (with kamatz) — 'Father who is merciful' / 'Father, the merciful One' — the kamatz indicates אָב is a noun in apposition (vocative), not a construct. This is used in אָבִינוּ אָב הָרַחֲמָן. (2) אַב הָרַחֲמִים (with patach) — 'Father OF mercy' — the patach makes it a סְמִיכוּת (construct state), meaning 'Father of mercies.' Artscroll uses the patach form for אַב הָרַחֲמִים הִטִיבָה בִרְצוֹנְךָ; Chabad uses kamatz. The vowel choice is not a trivial variant — it genuinely changes the grammatical relationship between the words.

**Examples:**

- אָב הָרַחֲמָן = vocative ('Father, the merciful One')
- אַב הָרַחֲמִים = construct ('Father of mercy')

**Sources:**

- ~ Ezra Arovas #msg4883
- ~ Ezra Arovas #msg4886
- ~ Avi Herzog #msg4887
- ~ Avi Herzog #msg4888

**See also:**

- [If a ba'al koreh reads the wrong vowel (e.g. a sheva instead of a patach), does that change the meaning?](#vowel-mistake-meaning-change)

### 140. What is the correct punctuation/commas in Birkas Kohanim as recited in davening — specifically the 'meshuleshet' issue?

Birkas Kohanim (the priestly blessing) has had much discussion about where commas and pauses belong, particularly in the third blessing (יברכך...). The main debate points:

1. The 'meshuleshet' (triplet) issue: whether the third blessing has a tripartite structure creating specific pause positions. Different editions show different comma placements.

2. The phrase 'עם קדושך' before 'כאמור': There should be a definite pause between 'עם קדושך' and 'כאמור' because 'כאמור' refers back to the bracha theme, not forward from 'עם קדושך'. This is supported by the ta'amei emet (cantillation of Psalms/Proverbs/Job) which show 'כאמור' as beginning a new phrase.

3. Manuscript and minhag differences: What appears in some siddurim as the correct phrasing may not follow the trop of the original passuk (Bamidbar 6:24-26). Ba'alei tefilla should consult the trop in Chumash for the original birkas kohanim to guide their phrasing.

Rav Breuer (in Da'at Mikra) discusses the ta'amei emet of the relevant passages, and different minhagim handle these pauses differently.

**Examples:**

- יברכך ה' וישמרך — pause before וישמרך or read as one unit? Different minhagim differ
- עם קדושך / כאמור — pause between these two; כאמור starts a new phrase

**Sources:**

- ~ Σliezer Bulka #msg16615
- Avi Block #msg16616
- ~ MG #msg16618
- ~ MG #msg16619

**See also:**

- [How should שוכן עד מרום וקדוש שמו be parsed in davening — does the punctuation differ between Shabbos and Yom Tov?](#shochen-ad-parsing)
- [Where should one pause in common tefilla phrases like מלך אוהב צדקה ומשפט, שומר עמו ישראל לעד, and זה א-לי ענו ואמרו?](#tefilla-punctuation)

### 141. Why is there a chatzi kaddish after Shabbos/weekday leining, and why is there NO kaddish after leining at Mincha?

The חֲצִי קַדִּישׁ serves as a מַפְסִיק (separator) between major sections of davening (the Abudarham appears to be the first source for this explanation). After Shabbos leining, the kaddish separates the kri'ah from the rest of the service. At Mincha, however, the kri'ah is followed almost immediately by חֲצִי קַדִּישׁ before שְׁמוֹנֶה עֶשְׂרֵה, so saying another kaddish right after the kri'ah would be redundant. On Shabbos, Chabad does not even say Hodu Al Eretz and LeDavid Mizmor before Musaf, so the Musaf kaddish follows directly after the kri'ah without an intervening one. This is why someone who tries to say kaddish after shlishi on Chol HaMoed (before mincha) is quickly stopped — there is no need for it.

**Examples:**

- Shabbos: kaddish after leining ✓
- Mincha: no kaddish after leining (redundant before shmoneh esreh)
- Chol HaMoed shlishi: no kaddish either

**Sources:**

- ~ DL #msg3014
- ~ Dror Maor #msg3015
- ~ Dror Maor #msg3018
- ~ Avi Herzog #msg3020

### 142. Is Kol Nidrei a neder (vow) annulment, a hataras nedarim, or something else? And why does it have such a distinctive tune?

Kol Nidrei declares all vows made 'from this Yom Kippur to the next' as null and void. There is extensive discussion about:

1. **Legal mechanism**: Is it a retroactive annulment (hataras nedarim/anullment) or a conditional neder? The Ba'alei HaTosafos and the Ran debate whether it works at all — if the neder was made without the condition, how can retracting it work? Some say it only applies to nedarim that one will make in the future (opposite of the plain text).

2. **Tune**: The famous tune is not just for drama — it reflects the gravity of standing before Hashem and the need to be released from unfulfilled vows. Different communities have different versions of the tune, but all share a plaintive, drawn-out quality.

3. **Text**: 'From this Yom Kippur to the next Yom Kippur' — the Geonim already questioned how you can be matir a neder that hasn't been made yet, leading to textual variants where some say 'from the previous Yom Kippur.'

4. **Modern practice**: In most shuls, the chazzan recites Kol Nidrei three times to establish it halachically (beis din requirement for hataras nedarim). The congregation stands throughout.

For ba'alei k'riah, Kol Nidrei is interesting because:
- The trope-like chanting is one of the most recognizable nuscha'os in the year
- The bracha before Kol Nidrei (על דעת המקום) has a unique tune
- It's one of the few times the chazzan uses a non-standard nusach that is universally followed

**Examples:**

- Kol Nidrei tune — one of the most recognizable nuscha'os in the Jewish year
- Declares vows null 'from this Yom Kippur to the next' — debate about future vs past
- Recited 3 times to fulfill beis din requirement

### 143. What is the rarest Shemoneh Esrei? What is the rarest aliyah in the Torah reading cycle?

**Rarest Shemoneh Esrei:**
- Motza'ei Shabbos Chanukah Rosh Chodesh with ותן ברכה before December 4 (in Chutz La'Aretz)
- This combines: weekday maariv (Motza'ei Shabbos), Chanukah additions (יעלה ויבוא), Rosh Chodesh additions, AND the seasonal switch to ותן ברכה (winter request for dew/rain)
- Only occurs in Chutz La'Aretz where these factors align; in Eretz Yisrael the calendar is different
- Last occurrence: ~1994. Next: ~2089.
- Also rare: a non-leap year version compounds the rarity

**Rarest Aliyah:**
- A Chazak aliyah when Pekudei falls on Parshas Shekalim and Rosh Chodesh Adar Sheini
- The group debated: Shishi Chazak (6th aliyah ending Chazak)? No, that's not the rarest
- The rarest is more nuanced and depends on the specific calendar combination

**Other rare trivia discussed:**
- The six columns in the Torah that don't start with a vav
- Which Nevi'im have no haftarah
- Parshios Vayeitzei and Miketz have no petuchah or stumah (open/closed paragraph breaks)
- Which pesukim are missing individual words due to tikkunei soferim

For ba'alei k'riah, these are fun trivia but rarely practical. If you ever encounter the Motza'ei Shabbos Chanukah Rosh Chodesh combination, congratulations — you've had the rarest possible Shemoneh Esrei in your lifetime.

**Examples:**

- Rarest Shemoneh Esrei: Motza'ei Shabbos + Chanukah + Rosh Chodesh + V'sen Tal U'Matar (before Dec 4)
- Next occurrence: ~2089 (last: 1994)
- Rarest aliyah debated: Shishi Chazak on specific calendar quirks

**Sources:**

- ~ Gilad #msg3304
- ~ Aharon Gaffen #msg3307
- ~ Gedaliah Levin #msg3312
- ~ MG #msg3316

### 144. Is the blessing 'שהכל נהיה בדברו' pronounced with a kamatz or segol under the yud of נהיה? What do the major poskim say?

There is a known machlokes about the nikkud of the word נהיה in the bracha 'שהכל נהיה בדברו':

1. **Segol under the yud** (נֶהְיֶה) — this is a nif'al participle (present tense), meaning 'is brought into being'. This form matches most other brachos which use present tense (e.g., בורא, נותן). This is the view of the Chachmas Menuchah, quoted by the Magen Avraham.

2. **Kamatz under the yud** (נֶהְיָה) — this would be a nif'al past tense ('was brought into being'). The Magen Avraham (OC 167:8) ultimately paskens that it should be with a kamatz. He mentions that the segol form has an additional meaning problem: it could also be read as the first-person plural kal future ('we will become'). The Pri Megadim and Machatzis HaShekel explain further.

3. The Gra (Vilna Gaon) said it with a segol (Maaseh Rav §76).

The Mishnah Berurah does not address this explicitly. The Pri Megadim leans toward kamatz.

**Practical ruling**: The majority of published siddurim used in Ashkenazi shuls today use the kamatz version. Some have a custom to say it with a segol. Either is acceptable since it is a rabbinic bracha, but ba'alei k'riah and those particular about dikduk should be aware that the kamatz form has stronger poskim support (Magen Avraham, Pri Megadim) despite the segol being grammatically cleaner (matching the present tense of other brachos).

**Examples:**

- נֶהְיָה (kamatz) = past tense, 'was brought into being' — supported by Magen Avraham
- נֶהְיֶה (segol) = present participle, 'is brought into being' — supported by Chachmas Menuchah and Gra

**Sources:**

- ~ Dror Maor #msg2625
- ~ MJ #msg2626
- ~ MJ #msg2628
- ~ Ephraim Slepoy #msg2632
- ~ MJ #msg2633

### 145. How should שוכן עד מרום וקדוש שמו be parsed in davening — does the punctuation differ between Shabbos and Yom Tov?

There is a well-known difference in how this phrase from Yeshayahu 57:15 is chanted:

- On Shabbos, many chazzanim run the words together: שוכן עד מרום, וקדוש שמו ('He dwells until exalted, and His name is holy'). This is widely considered a misreading — it treats מרום as modifying עד rather than וקדוש.
- On Yom Tov, it is correctly parsed as: שוכן עד, מרום וקדוש שמו ('He dwells forever, His name is exalted and holy').

The correct parsing, supported by the trop in the verse (ישעיהו נז, טו), treats the phrase as three statements: (1) שוכן עד, (2) מרום וקדוש שמו, (3) and then the continuation. The pasuk reads: כִּי כֹה אָמַר רָם וְנִשָּׂא שֹׁכֵן עַד וְקָדוֹשׁ שְׁמוֹ מָרוֹם וְקָדוֹשׁ אֶשְׁכּוֹן... — the trop places a zakef on עד, indicating a pause before מרום.

A practical solution for Shabbos: use the regular nusach but insert a deliberate pause between עד and מרום, creating the phrase 'שוכן עד, מרום וקדוש שמו' even within the Shabbos melody.

**Examples:**

- שוכן עד מרום וקדוש שמו on Shabbos = misread as 'dwells until exalted'
שוכן עד, מרום וקדוש שמו on Yom Tov = correctly 'dwells forever, exalted and holy is His name'
- The passuk: כִּי כֹה אָמַר רָם וְנִשָּׂא שֹׁכֵן עַד וְקָדוֹשׁ שְׁמוֹ מָרוֹם וְקָדוֹשׁ אֶשְׁכּוֹן (Yeshayahu 57:15)

**Sources:**

- ~ Dror Maor #msg1133
- ~ R Abraham #msg1137
- ~ R Abraham #msg1150

**See also:**

- [Where should one pause in common tefilla phrases like מלך אוהב צדקה ומשפט, שומר עמו ישראל לעד, and זה א-לי ענו ואמרו?](#tefilla-punctuation)

### 146. When reciting the brachos before/after reading a megillah or other sefer, do you use the trop of what you are about to read?

Some people do: they cantillate the brachos in the tune of the leining that follows (e.g. the bracha before leining Koheles in Koheles trop). However, this is not universal. When reciting a bracha before a haftarah, many do use the haftarah trop melody. The bracha itself has a standard trop pattern: pashta-munach-zakef / tipcha-munach-etnachta / mahapach-pashta-zakef / tipcha-mercha-sof pasuk. On the Yomim Noraim, some olim cantillate the brachos in the same tune as the leining. In Chutz La'Aretz, some do not make the bracha at all before leining Koheles outside of Purim — a matter of minhag.

**Sources:**

- ~ Aharon Gaffen #msg6306
- ~ MJ #msg6307
- ~ Dror Maor #msg6308
- ~ Donny Rose #msg6309
- ~ Allen Schreier #msg6311

### 147. Where should one pause in common tefilla phrases like מלך אוהב צדקה ומשפט, שומר עמו ישראל לעד, and זה א-לי ענו ואמרו?

Many tefilla phrases are routinely mispunctuated by chazzanim. The key is that smaller words (like לעד, ואמרו) frequently modify what follows rather than what precedes them:

- מלך אוהב צדקה ומשפט, רופא חולי עמו ישראל — the pause should be before ישראל, not after it (ישראל goes with the previous phrase, not the next).
- שומר עמו ישראל לעד — לעד goes back on שומר (He guards His people Israel forever), so the pause should be before ישראל.
- זה א-לי ענו ואמרו — the pause is before ענו, not before ואמרו. ואמרו goes forward with the next phrase (ואמרו ה' ימלוך לעולם ועד).

Other common examples:
- אל תעזבינו ה' א-להינו אל תרחק ממנו — pause after ה', not after א-להינו.
- ברוך אתה ה' א-להי ישראל אבינו מעולם ועד עולם — pause after אבינו.
- ונקיתי דמם לא נקיתי — the pause changes the meaning: 'I will cleanse [them], their blood I will NOT cleanse' vs running it together.

Ba'alei tefilla should look up the trop in the source passuk for any phrase quoted from Tanach — the trop tells you exactly where the pauses belong.

**Examples:**

- מלך אוהב צדקה ומשפט / רופא חולי עמו ישראל — pause before ישראל, not after 'amo'
- שומר עמו ישראל לעד — לעד modifies שומר, pause before ישראל
- זה א-לי ענו ואמרו — pause before ענו, ואמרו goes forward (ואמרו ה' ימלוך)

**Sources:**

- ~ MG #msg5955
- ~ MG #msg5957
- ~ MJ #msg5963
- ~ MJ #msg5966
- ~ MJ #msg5973

**See also:**

- [How should שוכן עד מרום וקדוש שמו be parsed in davening — does the punctuation differ between Shabbos and Yom Tov?](#shochen-ad-parsing)

### 148. On Tisha b'Av morning, why is kaddish said BEFORE the haftarah instead of after?

This is a longstanding puzzle. Normally, whenever the acharon (last oleh) reads the haftarah, the kaddish comes after the haftarah. Tisha b'Av is the exception where kaddish precedes it. One explanation: שְׁלִישִׁי is also מַפְטִיר by שַׁחֲרִית on Tisha b'Av, which may affect the ordering. The order on Tisha b'Av night is: kaddish after leining, then Aleinu. During the day, some communities (including Sefardim in some versions) may say kaddish after the first keri'ah as well, though this varies.

**Sources:**

- Rabbi Zvi Freund #msg3034
- Rabbi Zvi Freund #msg3035
- Rabbi Zvi Freund #msg3036
- ~ אקדמות מילין #msg3041
- Rabbi Zvi Freund #msg3044

## Sources, Sefaria & Gilyonot

### 149. What is meant by a 'better manuscript' when discussing trop/nikkud differences? Which manuscripts are authoritative?

When ba'alei k'riah and dikduk experts refer to a 'better manuscript' (כתב יד טוב יותר), they are referring to manuscripts that are closer to the original Masoretic tradition transmitted from the Tiberian Masoretes (Ben Asher family).

The hierarchy of manuscript authority:

1. **Keter Aram Tzova (Aleppo Codex)** — the gold standard. Written by Shlomo ben Buya'a and annotated by Aharon Ben Asher himself. Considered the most accurate witness to the Ben Asher tradition for both nikkud and trop. Housed in Jerusalem, partially preserved.

2. **Codex Leningradensis (Leningrad Codex, St. Petersburg Codex)** — the oldest complete dated manuscript (1008 CE). Used as the base for Biblia Hebraica and most critical editions. Slightly less authoritative than the Keter but the oldest complete text.

3. **Other early manuscripts** — various Cairo Geniza fragments, early Sephardi manuscripts, etc.

4. **Printed editions** (Venice Bomberg, Mikraot Gedolot) — incorporate later scribal traditions and corrections, less authoritative for individual trop/nikkud decisions.

For contemporary use:
- **Da'at Mikra** (Mossad Harav Kook) — follows the Keter / Ben Asher tradition
- **Koren / Breuer** — based on Keter and Ben Asher manuscripts
- **Simanim** — generally follows Ben Asher but makes some different choices

Ba'alei k'riah should be aware that common chumashim (Artscroll, etc.) sometimes use printed editions rather than manuscript-based traditions for trop. When there's a question about a specific trop mark or nikkud, consulting a manuscript-based edition (Breuer, Keter-based) is the most reliable approach.

**Examples:**

- Keter Aram Tzova (Aleppo Codex) — the most authoritative manuscript, annotated by Ben Asher himself
- Codex Leningradensis (1008 CE) — oldest complete manuscript, used for Biblia Hebraica / BHQ
- Da'at Mikra, Koren/Breuer — modern editions based on manuscript tradition vs Artscroll based on printed editions

**Sources:**

- ~ Sammy Noe #msg7514
- ~ Ezra Arovas #msg7515

**See also:**

- [Who divided the Tanach into chapters (perakim), and are the divisions Jewish in origin?](#perakim-origin)

### 150. What are the best sources for learning Biblical Hebrew dikduk and grammar? Are there recommended sefarim or textbooks?

The group recommended several excellent resources for learning dikduk:

**Classic works:**
1. **Gesenius' Hebrew Grammar** — the most comprehensive reference work on Biblical Hebrew grammar. Still relevant ~200 years later (because Biblical Hebrew hasn't changed). Available online for free.
2. **Tuv Ta'am** by R' Eliyahu Bachur — the classic work on trop and its musical/logical structure
3. **Minchat Shai** — discusses masoretic variations in nikkud and trop with explanations

**Modern textbooks:**
1. **'The First Hebrew Primer'** (EKS Publishing) — good for absolute beginners, but not comprehensive
2. **'Hebrew for the Rest of Us'** (Lee Fields) — accessible overview
3. **YU textbook** (by Dr. Moshe Sokolow or similar) — used in Yeshiva University's Hebrew program

**Online resources:**
- Sefaria for looking up pesukim with nikkud
- Mechon Mamre for the Aleppo Codex tradition
- dikdukian.weeklyshtikle.com for practical ba'al k'riah dikduk

**Works specific to ba'alei k'riah:**
- **Torat HaKore** by R' Shlomo Glick — the definitive work on laws and dikduk for ba'alei k'riah
- **Sha'arei Efrayim** — practical halachos for kri'as haTorah
- **Yeivin** on Keter Aram Tzova — discusses manuscript-based nikkud and trop decisions

For someone starting out, the group recommended beginning with a basic textbook (like the YU one) and then moving to Gesenius for deeper reference. For practical kri'ah issues, Torat HaKore is the go-to source.

**Examples:**

- Gesenius' Hebrew Grammar — most comprehensive reference
- Torat HaKore — definitive work for ba'alei k'riah
- Tuv Ta'am (R' Eliyahu Bachur) — classic on trop structure
- Minchat Shai — masoretic variations explained

**Sources:**

- ~ Rabbi Rosenberg #msg3663
- ~ Donny Rose #msg3664

**See also:**

- [What is meant by a 'better manuscript' when discussing trop/nikkud differences? Which manuscripts are authoritative?](#better-manuscripts)
- [Who divided the Tanach into chapters (perakim), and are the divisions Jewish in origin?](#perakim-origin)

### 151. In כִּכָּר זָהָב טָהוֹר (Shemos 25:39), does the word כִּכָּר have a kamatz or a patach under the kaf? Which is more accurate and why do editions differ?

— ככר, וזהב טהר'
- MJ suggested that 'זהב טהור' is being used as an adjective, which would explain the kamatz

**Torat HaKore's guidance:**
As a general rule from the group: when Torat HaKore gives a first option, and that option has Breuer's printing and more manuscripts backing it up, go with that option. Here: kamatz.

**For ba'alei k'riah:**
- The accurate reading is with a kamatz (kikar)
- Most ta'amei hamikra experts follow kamatz
- If your tikkun shows patach, you may want to correct it based on the manuscript evidence
- Sefaria had a debate about this and changed their text — a testament to how debated this question is

כִּכַּ֛ר (patach) — Venice/Mikraot Gedolot, Koren, some printed editions
- Rule of thumb: when Torat HaKore's first option + Breuer + major mss agree, go with that

YouTube has various teachers. The mgketer program (no longer available for sale, but downloadable with effort) can display trop analysis per word. For ta'amei emet specifically: MG recommends several works (specific titles not recalled in the thread). A tip from a seasoned BK: record yourself leining from the chumash, then play it back while leining from the tikkun — it prevents errors from getting stuck in your head.

### 153. When different manuscripts have different nikkud for the same word (like הסרפד with kamatz vs patach), how is the correct version determined?

When there's a disagreement between manuscripts on nikkud, the following methodology is used:

1. **Check the oldest and most authoritative manuscripts first**: Keter Aram Tzova (Aleppo Codex) is the gold standard. If the Keter has a reading, that's generally accepted as correct — as in the case of הסרפד, where the Keter has a patach.

2. **Define the grammatical issue**: Is the word in construct (semichut) or absolute form? A patach often indicates semichut (construct), while a kamatz can indicate absolute form. For הסרפד, grammatically a patach would suggest it's in semichut, but there's nothing to attach it to — some nouns just end in patach (like אֶצְבַּע, צְלָצַל, חַשְׁמַל).

3. **Check the context**: Does the nikkud make sense with the word's function in the pasuk?

4. **Consider manuscript families**:
   - Keter (Ben Asher) vs Leningrad (Ben Asher tradition but later)
   - Sephardi manuscripts vs Ashkenazi manuscripts
   - Printed editions (Venice, Mikraot Gedolot) vs manuscript-based editions

5. **Check a range of reliable editions**:
   - Breuer (based on Keter)
   - Da'at Mikra (follows Keter)
   - Koren (generally follows Ben Asher)
   - Simanim (has detailed he'arot)

6. **When conflicting traditions exist**: Both versions may be legitimate alternatives. The ba'al k'riah should follow their shul's minhag or the most authoritative manuscript for the specific word.

In the case of הסרפד, the Keter has a patach, so modern manuscript-based editions (Breuer, Da'at Mikra) follow that. Older printed editions had a kamatz, which was likely a printer's choice.

**Examples:**

- הסרפד — Keter has patach; older printed editions had kamatz; modern editions follow Keter
- Methodology: Keter first, then grammar, then context, then other manuscripts

**Sources:**

- ~ Yosef #msg2957
- ~ MJ #msg2963
- ~ MG #msg2965
- ~ MG #msg2971

**See also:**

- [What is meant by a 'better manuscript' when discussing trop/nikkud differences? Which manuscripts are authoritative?](#better-manuscripts)

### 154. Why do some editions like Koren and Simanim put a meteg (ga'ya) under a letter where the better manuscripts don't have one? How should I decide which to follow?

Different editions of Tanach use different methodologies for placing metegs:

**Why editions differ:**
- Koren and Simanim often add metegs based on GRAMMATICAL RULES rather than manuscript evidence
- The better manuscripts (Aleppo Codex, Leningrad Codex, Sassoon) only have metegs where the Masoretic tradition actually placed them
- Rabbi Breuer's edition follows the manuscripts strictly — if a meteg isn't in the mss, it's not in Breuer
- Simanim in particular has changed over time, getting closer to Breuer with each new edition

**MG's critique of Simanim:**
- 'This is part of the reason why I insist that Simanim is not reliable. They don't outline their methodology and every edition changes somewhat'
- MJ added: 'Simanim in particular are always changing and every new edition becomes closer and closer to Breuer, torat hakorei etc.'

**The practical impact:**
- A meteg under a kamatz tells you it's kamatz GADOL (long 'a'), not katan
- A meteg can change whether a following shva is na or nach
- Different meteg placement can therefore change the pronunciation

**Why some editions add meteg 'by rule':**
- Some editors believe that if the grammatical rules say a meteg SHOULD be there, they add it even if the manuscripts don't have it
- R' Breuer's approach: the manuscripts are the primary evidence, not grammatical theories

**For ba'alei k'riah:**
- If you want the most accurate text, follow R' Breuer's edition or Torat HaKore
- If you use Simanim or Koren, be aware that some metegs are 'editorial' — added by rule, not from manuscripts
- When in doubt about a specific meteg, check a manuscript-based edition (Breuer, Keter)
- The group consensus: manuscript evidence trumps grammatical rules when they conflict

**Examples:**

- Koren/Simanim: add metegs by grammatical rule (even where mss lack them)
- Breuer/Keter: only include metegs attested in manuscripts
- Simanim changes over time — newer editions closer to Breuer
- MG: 'I insist Simanim is not reliable — they don't outline their methodology'

**Sources:**

- ~ Avi Herzog #msg15833
- ~ MG #msg15834
- ~ MJ #msg15835
- ~ MG #msg15836

**See also:**

- [What is the difference between the terms ga'ya, meteg, ma'arich, and ma'amid?](#gaya-meteg-terminology)
- [What is the practical function of a meteg/ga'ya for a ba'al k'riah? When is it essential to pay attention to it?](#meteg-gaya-guide)
- [Does a meteg (ga'ya) indicate that the following shva is na?](#meteg-indicates-shva-na)

### 155. Who divided the Tanach into chapters (perakim), and are the divisions Jewish in origin?

The chapter divisions in Tanach are not Jewish in origin. They were introduced by Stephen Langton, the Archbishop of Canterbury, in the 13th century for the Latin Vulgate. Jewish manuscripts and printed editions before this period do not have chapter divisions as we know them today.

This is why chapter divisions sometimes cut across aliyos or create unnatural breaks in the Torah text. The Yiddish word 'kapitel' (chapter) comes from the Latin 'capitulum,' reflecting this Christian influence.

The Jewish tradition works with parshiyot (open and closed paragraph breaks) in the Torah and with the Masoretic division of books (24 books of Tanach). In Tehillim, manuscript evidence also shows no original chapter numbers — the current chapter divisions there are also later Christian additions, though the paragraph spacing in manuscripts often aligns with natural breaks.

The books of the Torah (the five chumashim) are Jewish in origin, as is the division of the Nevi'im and Ketuvim into 24 books. Only the internal chapter numbers within each book are the later addition.

**Examples:**

- Bereishis chapter 1 ends mid-story — a Christian chapter break, not a Jewish one
- Tehillim chapter divisions are not found in Jewish manuscripts; paragraph spacing aligns differently

**Sources:**

- ~ Σliezer Bulka #msg8610
- ~ MG #msg8612
- ~ MG #msg8613
- ~ MG #msg8623
- ~ Sammy Noe #msg8624

### 156. What exactly is the mitzvah of שְׁנַיִם מִקְרָא וְאֶחָד תַּרְגּוּם, and how should one fulfill it?

The mitzvah is to read the weekly parsha twice and the Targum (Onkelos) once. If one just reads the pesukim twice and rushes through the Targum, they are technically יוֹצֵא, but for Rashi one must actually understand what Rashi is saying — it cannot just be 'davened through' like a speed-reading ritual. A practical tip: if you learned the parsha with Rashi a few days prior (e.g. in a chabura), the weekly review goes much more quickly. The mitzvah applies to each parsha in its week; on weeks with combined parshiyos, both are read.

**Sources:**

- ~ Dror Maor #msg5090
- ~ Yoir #msg5091

**See also:**

- [Which edition of Targum Onkelos is considered best / most accurate?](#targum-onkelos-editions)

### 157. Which edition of Targum Onkelos is considered best / most accurate?

The תּוֹרַת חַיִּים edition is generally considered the best — it is based on good manuscripts. One important distinction: in Targum, the prefix דַּ (the Aramaic equivalent of the Hebrew relative אֲשֶׁר) is always a SEPARATE word when vocalized as דִּי (with a chirik), and a PREFIX (attached to the following word) when vocalized otherwise. The correct vocalization follows the same rules as Hebrew prefixes: ordinarily sheva, chirik before a shva nach, longer vowel before a guttural, etc. The older text of Targum Yonasan on the Chumash actually post-dates the Gemara, despite the attribution to Yonasan ben Uziel (who only wrote a Targum on Nevi'im).

**Sources:**

- ~ MJ #msg1428
- ~ Yoir #msg1429
- ~ MJ #msg1430
- ~ Yisroel Gradman #msg18187
- ~ Mordechai Kornbluth #msg18189
- ~ MG #msg18190

**See also:**

- [Who are the ba'alei mesora (Masoretes), and what exactly did they do?](#baalei-mesorah-who)

## Practical Ba'alei K'riah

### 158. What is the special tune for Az Yashir (Shiras HaYam)? How do ba'alei k'riah handle the conflict between the special tune and following the trop accurately?

Az Yashir (Shemos 15) is traditionally read with a special, more elaborate tune distinct from standard Torah trop. This creates a well-known tension between the melody and the trop:

**The problem**: The special tune often doesn't sync perfectly with the trup. For example, in some versions of the tune, every pasuk is split into two halves at the same melodic point, regardless of whether that point is an etnachta, zakef, or tipcha. This means you may be pausing at a tipcha with the same weight as an etnachta, which conflates the trop hierarchy.

**Approaches taken by ba'alei k'riah:**
1. **Follow the tune, ignore trop inconsistencies** — most common; the special niggun is the mesorah, and the congregation expects it
2. **Follow the trop strictly** — pause only at the strongest mafsik in each pasuk, and vary the cadence accordingly (different endings for etnachta vs zakef vs tipcha)
3. **Hybrid** — sing the special tune but be mafsik at the correct places, even if it means the melody doesn't resolve cleanly

**The same issue** exists for the tune of the Masa'os (Bamidbar 33) and the Nesi'im (Bamidbar 7).

The majority minhag is to use a special tune for Az Yashir, acknowledging that the trop accuracy is somewhat compromised for the sake of the mesorah/niggun. Ba'alei k'riah should be consistent within their own reading and follow their shul's custom.

**Examples:**

- Az Yashir (Shemos 15) — most famous example of special tune conflicting with trop
- Same issue: Masa'os (Bamidbar 33) and Nesi'im (Bamidbar 7) — both have special 'marching' tunes
- Some ba'alei k'riah follow the tune; others follow the trop strictly

**Sources:**

- Rabbi Zvi Freund #msg3908
- ~ MG #msg3909
- ~ Ezra Arovas #msg3910

**See also:**

- [Why do some ba'alei k'riah use a special tune for the Nesi'im (tribal princes) in Parshas Naso? Is it the same as the tune for Az Yashir or the Masa'os?](#nesiim-special-tune)
- [Why does the Masa'os (the journeys in Masei, Bamidbar 33) get a special tune? Is it the same as the Nesi'im tune?](#masaos-special-tune)

### 159. What is the origin of the custom for a Bar Mitzvah boy to read Maftir and Haftarah? And is Maftir a distinguished aliyah?

The custom for a Bar Mitzvah boy to read Maftir and Haftarah developed as a practical rite of passage. Historically, the Bar Mitzvah boy was called up for his first aliyah on the Shabbos after turning 13, and reading the haftarah (which requires brachos and can be done by a non-Kohen/Levi) became the natural culminating honor. The earliest Bar Mitzvah celebrations involved the boy reading from the Torah — the specific focus on Maftir + Haftarah emerged in medieval Ashkenazi practice.

Contrary to modern perception, Maftir is technically not the most distinguished aliyah. It is a 'supplementary' aliyah — the required seven aliyos are the main ones, and Maftir was added so that the haftarah could be read from a navi scroll. However, in common practice, Maftir has become seen as a prominent honor because it includes both the Torah reading and the haftarah with its brachos. The Bar Mitzvah boy reading Maftir is a minhag that varies by community — some call the boy up for a regular aliyah instead.

**Examples:**

- Most Ashkenazi shuls: Bar Mitzvah boy reads Maftir + Haftarah
Some Sephardi/Eretz Yisrael minhagim: boy gets an aliyah during the main seven
- Maftir was created so someone could read from the Navi scroll — it's a separate 'bonus' aliyah, not one of the required seven

### 160. If someone tries to differentiate between letters (e.g. ח/כ) authentically but sometimes slips, is that worse than never making any distinction at all (bedi'avad)?

The prevailing view: if you don't think you can be consistent, it is better to read ח like a כ in general than to attempt a distinction you can't maintain. If you are confident you can consistently differentiate, that is a different story. The underlying logic: an inconsistent distinction can sound like a mistake (as if you mispronounced), whereas a consistent non-distinction is simply the accepted bedi'avad practice most communities accept. Related: there is concern (intuitive, with support from poskim on being מאריך too much in the ח of אחד vs the ד — מקלקל התבה) that taking a breath mid-word to draw out a sound (e.g. shaking lulav while elongating נא־ in אנא ה' הושיעה נא) may be a hefsek that splits the word.

**Examples:**

- Differentiating ח/כ — better consistent-undifferentiated than inconsistent

**Sources:**

- ~ R Abraham #msg398
- ~ Moshe Klausner #msg399
- ~ Baruch #msg875
- ~ Baruch #msg876
- ~ MJ #msg878

**See also:**

- [Should someone who can't carry a tune (or who is slow) be allowed to daven at the amud for a yahrzeit, or is it tircha d'tzibura?](#chiyuv-vs-baal-tefilla-tircha)

### 161. Does taking a breath in the middle of a word constitute a hefsek (interruption) that disqualifies it?

The consensus leans toward yes — it splits the word in two, which is problematic. The common example is someone shaking the lulav while singing אַנָּא ה׳ הוֹשִׁיעַה נָּא and drawing out the נָּא to get two directions of the lulav, thereby taking a breath mid-word. A source brought: poskim who say that being מְאָרִיךְ too much in the ח of אֶחָד instead of in the ד is a problem because it is מְקַלְקֵל הַתֵּבָה (distorts the word). By analogy, breathing mid-word should be a hefsek. One caveat: some argue this may fall under the category of קְרָא וְלֹא דַקְדֵּק (read but was not precise) rather than being מְעַכֵּב (invalidating).

**Examples:**

- אַנָּא ה׳ הוֹשִׁיעַה נָּא — drawing out נָּא while shaking lulav

**Sources:**

- ~ Baruch #msg875
- ~ Baruch #msg876
- ~ MB #msg877
- ~ MJ #msg878
- ~ Moish #msg879
- ~ Baruch #msg880

**See also:**

- [If someone tries to differentiate between letters (e.g. ח/כ) authentically but sometimes slips, is that worse than never making any distinction at all (bedi'avad)?](#bediavad-pronunciation-acceptance)

### 162. Should someone who can't carry a tune (or who is slow) be allowed to daven at the amud for a yahrzeit, or is it tircha d'tzibura?

This is a perennial debate in the group, with several positions: (1) The Gemara's bar on a categorically unqualified person (one who mixes up letters / can't pronounce Hebrew) clearly applies; beyond that there is room for judgment. (2) Tircha d'tzibura (delaying/bothering the congregation) IS a well-established halachic concept — someone markedly slower than the norm should be told, and if unable to speed up may be prevented from davening. (3) Against that, many people find davening on a yahrzeit deeply meaningful, and most who genuinely can't hold a tune are aware of it and do not WANT to daven but feel duty-bound for the עילוי נשמה; good middos would have the tzibbur be mevater on its enjoyment for the one day. (4) A middle position: enact a shul rule (seen in some kehillos, e.g. Hager's) that only the regular Shabbos ba'alei tefilla may daven on a yahrzeit. (5) Mussaf and Kabbolas Shabbos are less of a chiyuv and arguably fine for anyone, being short חזרת הש״ץ; if one is makpid even there, where to draw the line? (6) Some personally decline: e.g. one member with yahrzeit on Yom Tov will not daven der Amud because he can't sing well, to avoid סניגור נעשה קטיגור and tircha — he recites only kaddish after Aleinu. Practical note: a Rov who forbade a ba'al teshuva from davening (as not מרוצה לקהל due to weak reading) did so discreetly to avoid embarrassment. (Also: a person is never 'a chiyuv'; a person HAS a chiyuv — common phrasing notwithstanding.)

**Sources:**

- ~ Avi Herzog #msg337
- ~ MJ #msg338
- ~ Dror Maor #msg344

### 163. In a double parsha (e.g. Behar-Bechukosai), which aliyah do you combine the parshiyos at, when the usual revi'i won't work?

Normally there is a preference to combine at revi'i. But in a double parsha where you would end up ending the 7th aliyah too early (e.g. Bechukosai is short, so combining at revi'i would land you at the 6th pasuk of Bechukosai for the end of 7), the Biur Halacha says explicitly that you combine at shishi instead. General principle: the revi'i preference yields when it is structurally impossible. (Guest ba'alei koreh have reported variants: e.g. when Bechukosai was alone and aliyos needed readjustment, one shul combined the first two aliyos for the kohen, the BK took tochacha as Levi, and revi'i was split into two.)

**Examples:**

- Behar-Bechukosai: combine at shishi, not revi'i

**Sources:**

- ~ Aharon Gaffen #msg386
- Josh #msg387
- ~ Aharon Gaffen #msg378

### 164. Should the mapik heh be pronounced in Kaddish, or is it dropped in Aramaic?

Some people do not pronounce the mapik ה in kaddish (e.g. יִתְהַלַּל without the mapik on the ל, or omitting it elsewhere), claiming that in Aramaic the mapik is not pronounced. However, the prevailing view in the group is that this is not an official minhag or mesorah — there is no documented tradition to drop the mapik in Aramaic specifically. One should simply pronounce it correctly. That said, there is also concern about an overly 'performed' pronunciation — articulating the mapik with exaggerated emphasis ('like I'll show em') is inappropriate. The right approach is quiet consistency.

**Sources:**

- ~ MJ #msg4307
- ~ Moish #msg4308
- ~ MJ #msg4309
- ~ MB #msg4310
- ~ MJ #msg4311
- ~ Σliezer Bulka #msg4312

**See also:**

- [Does a mapik heh change the meaning of a word?](#mapik-heh-meaning-change)
- [Are there places where a mappik heh is expected but missing, and where can one find the lists?](#dagesh-mapik-omitted-list)

### 165. Why does the Masa'os (the journeys in Masei, Bamidbar 33) get a special tune? Is it the same as the Nesi'im tune?

The Masa'os (42 journeys of Bnei Yisrael in the desert, Bamidbar 33) is traditionally read with a special tune, similar to the one used for the Nesi'im (tribal princes) in Naso and Az Yashir (Shiras HaYam).

**Why a special tune?**
- It's a list — 42 repetitive entries about travel. The tune breaks the monotony.
- It's called a 'marching' tune, reflecting the travel/journey motif
- The mesorah (tradition) passed down includes this special niggun

**Does it conflict with trop?**
- Yes — same issue as Az Yashir. The special tune often doesn't respect the sof passuk, blending consecutive pesukim together. This is the biggest objection some ba'alei k'riah have with it.
- Some ba'alei k'riah address this by following the trop strictly while using a slightly altered rhythm rather than a full special tune
- Others use the special tune and accept the trop compromise as part of the mesorah

**Which parshiyos get this treatment?**
1. Az Yashir (Shemos 15) — Shirah
2. Nesi'im (Bamidbar 7) — 12 tribal princes
3. Masa'os (Bamidbar 33) — 42 journeys

Not all communities observe all three. Check your shul's minhag before doing any special tune.

**Examples:**

- Masa'os (Bamidbar 33) — 42 journeys with a special 'marching' tune
- Same tune used for: Az Yashir (Shemos 15) and Nesi'im (Bamidbar 7)
- Objection: the tune conflates pesukim, not respecting sof passuk

**Sources:**

- ~ Yoir #msg2046
- ~ Yechiel Pisem #msg2053
- ~ MG #msg2055

**See also:**

- [What is the special tune for Az Yashir (Shiras HaYam)? How do ba'alei k'riah handle the conflict between the special tune and following the trop accurately?](#az-yashir-tune)
- [Why do some ba'alei k'riah use a special tune for the Nesi'im (tribal princes) in Parshas Naso? Is it the same as the tune for Az Yashir or the Masa'os?](#nesiim-special-tune)

### 166. Why do some ba'alei k'riah use a special tune for the Nesi'im (tribal princes) in Parshas Naso? Is it the same as the tune for Az Yashir or the Masa'os?

The 12 days of the Nesi'im (Bamidbar 7) are traditionally read with a special 'marching' tune in many communities. The reasons given include:

1. The Nesi'im represent a travel/journey motif — each prince brought his offerings, similar to the Masa'os (journeys) in Masei, which also gets a special tune
2. It breaks the monotony of 12 nearly identical repetitions
3. Some associate it with the 'marching' tune because the Nesi'im led the camp in their travels

The tune is often described as the same or similar to the tune used for Az Yashir (Shiras HaYam) and the Masa'os. However, there's a notable objection: the Nesi'im tune doesn't always respect the sof passuk (end of verse), blending pesukim together in a way that conflicts with the trop. Some ba'alei k'riah deliberately avoid the special tune for this reason.

Minhag varies:
- Some do the tune for all 12 Nesi'im plus the concluding pesukim (13th portion)
- Some only do it for part of them
- Others (particularly Litvish/yeshivish circles) skip it entirely
- Yekkish (German) communities may have their own mesorah for it

If your shul has no strong tradition, follow the shul's custom or skip the tune to be safe. The key is consistency — don't switch mid-aliyah.

**Examples:**

- The Nesi'im in Naso (Bamidbar 7) — 12 nearly identical repetitions about each tribal prince's offering
- The tune is similar to the one for Masa'os (Bamidbar 33) and Az Yashir (Shemos 15)
- Objection: the tune conflates pesukim, not respecting the sof passuk divisions

**Sources:**

- ~ Yoir #msg2046
- ~ DL #msg2049
- ~ MG #msg2051
- ~ Yechiel Pisem #msg2053
- ~ MG #msg2055

**See also:**

- [What is the special tune for Az Yashir (Shiras HaYam)? How do ba'alei k'riah handle the conflict between the special tune and following the trop accurately?](#az-yashir-tune)
- [Why does the Masa'os (the journeys in Masei, Bamidbar 33) get a special tune? Is it the same as the Nesi'im tune?](#masaos-special-tune)

### 167. Is a break (revach/רווח) needed between the words 'בני ישראל' to avoid the yud sounding like part of the previous nun? And what are the general rules for revach bein had'vakim?

Revach bein had'vakim (space between the connected/doubled letters) is the concept of inserting a slight pause between two words where the last letter of the first word and the first letter of the second word are the same or could be elided together. The concern is that without a break, the two words would sound like one word with a dagesh chazak.

Regarding 'בני ישראל' (Bnei Yisrael):
- The yud of Yisrael is not a consonant in this context — it is a vowel letter (chirik malei)
- Therefore, there is no risk of confusing the yud with the final nun of 'bnei'
- Most authorities say NO break is needed between 'bnei' and 'Yisrael'
- This is handy information because many ba'alei k'riah unnecessarily pause here

General rules for when a break IS needed:
- When the first word ends with a consonant and the second starts with the same consonant
- When the first word ends with a letter that could be misread as the beginning of the next word
- When there's a dagesh chazak at the beginning of the second word that could get lost

Examples where breaks are needed: 'כלל לא', 'בבר־מצוה', etc. But the practical rule is: if the two letters can naturally be separated without changing the word, no break is needed.

**Examples:**

- בני ישראל — NO break needed (yud is a vowel letter, not a consonant)
- כלל לא — break needed to distinguish from 'kellalo'
- בבר־מצוה — break needed when same letter repeats across word boundary

**Sources:**

- ~ ~ #msg7202
- ~ Avi #msg7203
- ~ Ian Beider #msg7222
- ~ Moish #msg7223

### 168. Does the ba'al k'riah have to show the oleh (person called up) where he will be reading from? Is this halacha or minhag?

The question of whether the ba'al k'riah must physically indicate to the oleh where the aliyah starts is not a clear halacha but rather a matter of minhag and common practice.

**Sources and reasoning:**
- The Sha'arei Efrayim discusses that we show the oleh the beginning of the aliyah 'so that he knows what he is making a bracha on' (שידע על מה הוא מברך)
- By the same logic, one could argue for showing the end of the aliyah too — which is why some have the minhag to drape the tzitzis over the entire section to be read
- At the end of the aliyah, the oleh kisses the end and the beginning, so the after-bracha covers all that was read

**Common practices:**
- In most shuls, the ba'al k'riah or gabbai points to the first word of the aliyah before the oleh begins his bracha
- Some shuls also mark the end of the aliyah with a pointer or finger
- If the oleh can find the place himself, it's not strictly required — but it's considered courteous (mentschlich) toward the ba'al k'riah to have the oleh ready before saying Barchu

**Challenges:**
- For a very long aliyah, showing both start and end can be impractical
- Some ba'alei k'riah find it annoying when the oleh asks 'where?' every time, especially if they read there every week

**Bottom line**: There's no absolute halachic requirement, but it's a well-established minhag and considered proper derech eretz. The ba'al k'riah should point to the start of the aliyah, and the oleh should be attentive and ready.

**Examples:**

- Sha'arei Efrayim: showing the start so the oleh knows what he's blessing
- Some show both start and end (draping tzitzis over the section)
- Common minhag: gabbai/BK points to first word before the bracha

**Sources:**

- Binyomin (Ben) Adler #msg4178
- ~ Dror Maor #msg4180
- ~ . #msg4212

### 169. Do we need to show the oleh where the aliyah starts (and ends)?

Showing the oleh where the aliyah starts is rooted in the chiyuv to know what one is making a bracha on — so even though most kohanim can find Bereishis, we point to the place. This applies to perhaps half a dozen places in the Torah (notably the start of each sefer). Showing where the aliyah ENDS is a separate minhag kept by Chabad and other chasidic communities (some ask where the aliyah ends before saying ברכו); it is understood as helping the oleh prepare, not as a strict requirement.

**Sources:**

- ~ MG #msg4188
- ~ Yoir #msg4189
- ~ MG #msg4190

**See also:**

- [Should someone who can't carry a tune (or who is slow) be allowed to daven at the amud for a yahrzeit, or is it tircha d'tzibura?](#chiyuv-vs-baal-tefilla-tircha)

### 170. Who takes the aliyah of the tochacha (admonition), and why is the ba'al koreh often called up without being formally 'called'?

The minhag that the ba'al koreh takes the tochacha without being formally called up is so that it not appear that the congregation is singling out / 'talking to' anyone in particular. The Mishna Berura adds: in כי תבוא, if the ba'al koreh is a kohen, one should make seven aliyos before the tochacha and call the ba'al koreh for אחרון. Many have the minhag that the ba'al koreh is oleh without being called up at all. Note: this only works if the BK is eligible for that aliyah (e.g. if he were a Levi, it couldn't work for a kohen's aliyah). Some (e.g. the Rav, Rav Soloveitchik) were makpid to receive the tochacha because of its importance.

**Examples:**

- כי תבוא — if BK is kohen, make 7 aliyos before tochacha, BK takes אחרון

**Sources:**

- ~ MJ #msg907
- ~ YYC #msg1200
- ~ Dovid H #msg1311
- ~ Joseph Rosenbaum #msg1312
- ~ Avi Herzog #msg1314

**See also:**

- [Do we need to show the oleh where the aliyah starts (and ends)?](#showing-oleh-the-place)

### 171. In Kiddush, should ורצה בנו (vartzah banu) be pronounced with a mil'eil accent (VAR-tzah) due to nasog achor? Does it change the meaning?

Yes — ורצה (vartzah, 'and He desired') should be pronounced mil'eil (VAR-tzah) due to nasog achor, because the following word בָּנוּ (banu) is milra (or monosyllabic for NA purposes).

**Why NA applies:**
- ורצה is a mil'eil word normally (VAR-tzah, accent on first syllable)
- Wait — actually the question is whether NA makes it MIL'EIL (where it would normally be milra)
- The word ורצה (vartzah) normally has its accent on the last syllable (milra: var-TZAH)
- BUT when followed by a milra word (בָּנוּ, ba-NU), NA should retract the accent one syllable: VAR-tzah (mil'eil)
- This is the standard application of nasog achor

**Does it change the meaning?**
- Chaim Komarow asked: 'is it mileil due to NA or is it not since that would change the meaning?'
- MJ confirmed it IS mileil due to NA, giving a parallel example from Tanach: לֹא שָׁתָה מַיִם (haftara of Vayikra) where שתה is mil'eil due to NA
- Interestingly, even when it's milra, the word could still have the alternative meaning (feminine present tense of the ע״ו verb, as well as the intended masculine past tense of the ל״ה verb) — so the NA doesn't actually create ambiguity

**Group consensus:**
- Avi Herzog: 'I personally pronounce it nasog achor'
- Alexander Adler: 'I tend to agree'
- Reuven Levitin pointed out the same applies to 'אשר בחר בנו... ונתן לנו' in Birkas HaTorah

**For ba'alei k'riah:**
- Pronounce ורצה as VAR-tzah (mil'eil) in Kiddush due to NA
- The same principle applies to other similar phrases in davening
- The meaning ('and He desired us') is understood regardless of the accent placement

**Examples:**

- וְרָצָה בָנוּ → VAR-tzah banu (mil'eil due to NA)
- אֲשֶׁר בָּחַר בָּנוּ → BAH-char banu (same principle in Birkas HaTorah)
- לֹא שָׁתָה מַיִם → SHAH-tah mayim (parallel from Tanach, haftara of Vayikra)

**Sources:**

- ~ Chaim Komarow #msg17899
- ~ MJ #msg17900
- ~ Avi Herzog #msg17903
- Reuven Levitin #msg17905

**See also:**

- [What is nasog achor (נסוג אחור), and when a word 'retreats' its accent, why does it sometimes happen even when the next word looks milra?](#nasog-achor)
- [Why doesn't nasog achor apply to a word like וַיִּקַּח (vayikach)? What does a dagesh chazak have to do with accent retraction?](#nasog-achor-closed-syllable)
- [Why is וַיִּקַּח קֹרַח not subject to nasog achor (accent retraction) even though it appears to be mil'eil? Does the same apply in Sifrei Emet?](#vayikach-korach-nasog-achor)
